coredata.dc:title: Electrochemiluminescence revealing that HNO3-oxidized single-walled carbon nanotubes are essentially tubular graphene quantum dot-nanoassemblies
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ruina
Li, Ailing
Huang, Yun
Shi, Yanfang
Chi, Yuwu
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as recently emerging 0-D graphitic nanomaterials, have attracted much attention due to their unique optical, electrical and catalytic properties. However, some properties of GQDs, such as very small size, low density, and excellent water-solubility, make it difficult to be separated and immobilized. This may limit the recycle and purification of GQDs, and thus their applications in catalysis and sensing. In this work, we prepared tubular GQD-nanoassemblies (t-GQD-NAs) by etching single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) with concentrated HNO3. The synthesized t-GQD-NAs were characterized by TEM, SEM, XPS, Raman spectroscopy and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) in details. t-GQD-NAs were much shorter and slimmer in morphology, bore more oxygen-containing groups, and had a higher surface defect density compared with SWCNTs. t-GQD-NAs not only maintained good UV absorption property of SWCNTs but also showed strong infrared ECL emission and broad ECL spectrum, verifying that t-GQD-NAs were assembled from GQDs of various sizes and some graphene nanoribbons. This is for the first time that 0-D GQDs were reported to be assembled into 1-D tubular carbon structures. The t-GQD-NAs with abundant surface states and good ECL activity could be easily separated, purified, and immobilized, suggesting their promising applications in ECL sensing and catalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Single-walled carbon nanotubes
Graphene quantum dots
Nanoassembly

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Defects and oxygen-containing groups on t-GQD-NAs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedures of t-GQD-NAs from SWCNTs and their ECL emission on GCE.
TEM images obtained for raw SWCNTs (A); t-GQD-NAs-1 (B); t-GQD-NAs-2 (C); t-GQD-NAs-3 (D) at different resolutions with scale bars of 500 nm (a); 100 nm (b); and 20 nm (c), respectively.
SEM images obtained for raw SWCNTs (A); t-GQD-NAs-1 (B); t-GQD-NAs-2 (C); t-GQD-NAs-3 (D) with low (left column) and high (right column) resolution.
Raman spectra of raw SWCNTs and t-GQD-NAs: (a) raw SWCNTs; (b) t-GQD-NAs-1; (c) t-GQD-NAs-2; (d) t-GQD-NAs-3.
XPS survey spectra (A) and high-resolution XPS C1S spectra (B) obtained for raw SWCNTs (a); t-GQD-NAs-1 (b); t-GQD-NAs-2 (c); and t-GQD-NAs-3 (d).

Cyclic voltammograms (A) and curves of ECL intensity versus potential (B) obtained at bare GCE in the presence of K2S2O8 (green line), t-GQD-NAs-2-modified GC electrode in the absence of K2S2O8 (blue line), and t-GQD-NAs-2-modified GCE in the presence of K2S2O8 (red line). The supporting electrolyte was 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) and the potential scan rate was 0.2 V s−1.

ECL emission of t-GQD-NAs obtained by etching SWCNTs with 7.5 M HNO3 for different time: (Raw SWCNTs) 0 h; (t-GQD-NAs-1) 8 h; (t-GQD-NAs-2) 12 h; (t-GQD-NAs-3) 20 h. The electrolyte consisted of 0.1 M K2S2O8, 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution and the potential scan rate was 0.2 V s−1.

(A) Photos obtained for the t-GQD-NAs-2-modified GCE in bright field (a); in dark field without applied potential (b); and in dark field with applied scanning (0.2 V s−1) potential from 0.00 to −2.50 V. (B) ECL spectrum of t-GQD-NAs-2 in the presence of 0.2 M K2S2O8. The supporting electrolyte was 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0).

Schematic diagram of the possible structure of t-GQD-NAs, which are assembled from small-sized GQDs (I), medium-sized GQDs (II), large-sized GQDs (III), and graphene ribbons (IV) by SP3 carbon connections.

head.ce:title: Electrochemiluminescence revealing that HNO3-oxidized single-walled carbon nanotubes are essentially tubular graphene quantum dot-nanoassemblies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, and College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350108, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology
Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, and College of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, and College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350108, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Quanzhou Medical College, Quanzhou, Fujian 362011, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Quanzhou Medical College, Quanzhou, Fujian 362011, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as recently emerging 0-D graphitic nanomaterials, have attracted much attention due to their unique optical, electrical and catalytic properties. However, some properties of GQDs, such as very small size, low density, and excellent water-solubility, make it difficult to be separated and immobilized. This may limit the recycle and purification of GQDs, and thus their applications in catalysis and sensing. In this work, we prepared tubular GQD-nanoassemblies (t-GQD-NAs) by etching single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) with concentrated HNO3. The synthesized t-GQD-NAs were characterized by TEM, SEM, XPS, Raman spectroscopy and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) in details. t-GQD-NAs were much shorter and slimmer in morphology, bore more oxygen-containing groups, and had a higher surface defect density compared with SWCNTs. t-GQD-NAs not only maintained good UV absorption property of SWCNTs but also showed strong infrared ECL emission and broad ECL spectrum, verifying that t-GQD-NAs were assembled from GQDs of various sizes and some graphene nanoribbons. This is for the first time that 0-D GQDs were reported to be assembled into 1-D tubular carbon structures. The t-GQD-NAs with abundant surface states and good ECL activity could be easily separated, purified, and immobilized, suggesting their promising applications in ECL sensing and catalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphitic carbon nanomaterials mainly including single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs), multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), graphene, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are well known, extensively studied and widely applied carbon nanostructures in the past decades. As 1-D graphitic nanomaterials, MWCNTs and SWCNTs were relatively early discovered [1,2], however, they did not attract much attention until 1990s [3–5]. Nowadays, MWCNTs and SWCNTs with many excellent properties, such as many-fold stronger than steel [6–9], much harder than diamond [10], thermal conductivity higher than any bulk material [11,12], and electroconductivity significantly higher than copper [13–15], have been industrially produced [16], and widely applied in various fields such as, energy, transport, environment, sport, health, electronics, and sensing [17–21]. Graphene as a new kind of 2-D carbon nanomaterial has received much attention [22], shortly after it was first prepared in 2004 [23]. Graphene not only exhibits supreme electrical, thermal, optical and mechanical properties almost on the same level as MWCNTs and SWCNTs [24], but also has some obvious advantages over CNTs such as easy pattern formation, optical transparency, and electrochemistry [25,26]. Therefore, graphene has become one of the most extensively studied and applied nanomaterials in the past decade. In 2004, just the same year when graphene nanosheets were first reported, fluorescent 0-D carbon nanomaterials, namely carbon based dots (CDs) were first found and extracted from SWCNT fragments [27]. CDs are usually categorized as CQDs and GQDs [28]. The former are graphitic nanospheres with size less than 10 nm [29–33], while the latter refer to graphene sheets with lateral size less then 100 nm [34,35]. As emerging nanomatrials with unique optical, electrical, and catalytic properties, as well as excellent biocompatibility and low environmental toxicity [36], CDs have now attracted much attention in the fields of bioimaging [37,38], energy conversion and storage [39], sensor [28], and catalysis [40].

Due to quantum confinement effect and surface effect, CDs (0-D carbon) have shown various attractive optical properties (e.g. fluorescence, electrochemiluminescence, and chemiluminescence) [28], which enables CDs to have more attractive applications in the fields of sensing, photocatalysis, and bioimaging than CNTs (1-D carbon) and graphene (2-D carbon) [37–40]. Interestingly, these different types of graphitic carbon nanomaterials can be changed into one another under certain conditions. For examples, CNTs of 1-D carbon can be converted into graphene sheets of 2-D carbon by unzipping CNTs with Ar plasma [41]. Reversely, graphene can be rolled up into CNTs using selective atomic adsorption [42]. In terms of CDs of 0-D carbon, they can be prepared both from CNTs by chemical oxidation with nitric acid [27,43], and from graphene by microwave treatment [35]. However, to date it is very different to prepare CNTs (1-D) or graphene (2-D) from CDs (0-D), e.g. GQDs, probably due to the fact that CNTs and graphene sheets are much more ordered nanomaterials than CDs.

It would be of considerable research interest to assemble GQDs into “graphene sheets” or “CNTs”-shaped nanocomposites since they not only may inherit the excellent optical and catalytic properties from GQDs but also have the advantages of CNTs and graphene, such as strong mechanical strength, good conductivity and easy assembly. However, as mentioned above, it is almost impossible to obtain GQD assemblies with tube (1-D) or sheet (2-D) shapes. In order to overcome this issue, herein we use a strategy by which SWCNTs are etched with concentrated nitric acid (Eq. (1) and top section of Fig. 1 ) into tubular GQD- nanoassemblies (t-GQD-NAs), instead of the attempt to assemble GQDs into the tubular nanoassemblies directly. The obtained t-GQD-NAs basically maintain the tubular shape of SWCNTs, but consist of many GQDs connected with one another probably by SP3 carbons (e.g. CO, COH) or SP2 carbons (e.g. CC). t-GQD-NAs have strong electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [44,45] activity in the presence of peroxydisulfate (the bottom section of Fig. 1), showing a characteristic property of GQDs [35]. Moreover, 1-D t-GQD-NAs have excellent film-forming ability and can be easily immobilized on the electrode surface, as their parent nanomaterils (i.e. SWCNTs) do. t-GQD-NAs combine the attractive merits of GQDs (i.e. strong ECL emission) and SWCNTs (i.e. easy film forming and good electroconductivity), while overcome the shortcomings of GQDs (e.g. difficult assembly and loading) and SWCNTs (e.g. no visible light emission and relatively weak catalytic properties), making t-GQD-NAs become new and good 1-D electrochemiluminescent carbon nanomaterials. It is envisioned that t-GQD-NAs may have promising applications in chemical sensing, displaying devices, and catalysis.
(1)
C + 4HN
O 3
concentrated
→
h e a t
C
O 2
↑
+ 4N
O 2
↑
+ 2
H 2
O

The raw SWCNTs (>95% purity) with 1–2 nm diameters and 1–3 μm lengths were bought from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Concentrated HNO3 (68%) of analytical purity was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Potassium peroxodisulfate (99.5%) was received from Fuchen chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Other chemicals were all analytically pure or higher. All chemicals were used as received without further purification, and doubly distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

Target t-GQD-NAs were prepared by etching SWCNTs with concentrated HNO3. In a typical procedure, 30 mg of raw SWCNTs and 30 mL of 7.5 M HNO3 was in turn put into 100 mL round-bottomed flask, and the suspension was refluxed for 12 h in an oil bath (140 °C) under electromagnetic stirring. Then the resultant suspension was cooled to room temperature, diluted with 70 mL water and filtered with 0.2-μm filter membrane. The obtained residue was washed with water for several times until the filtrate became neutral. Finally, t-GQD-NA suspension was obtained after dispersing the purified residue in 10 mL water. The obtained t-GQD-NAs prepared by etching SWCNTs for 8, 12 and 20 h were respectively denoted as t-GQD-NAs-1, t-GQD-NAs-2 and t-GQDs-NAs-3 (Table S1).

A disk glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with a diameter of 3.0 mm was used as the working electrode for evaluating the ECL of t-GQD-NAs. Before a typical measurement, t-GQD-NAs were modified on the GCE with following procedure: The GCE was in turn polished with Al2O3 nanoparticles (50 nm), cleaned by water with the help of ultrasonic wave, and dried with a N2 gas stream for further modification. Subsequently, the t-GQD-NAs-modified GCE was obtained by dropping 5 μL of t-GQD-NA suspension on the surface of the above clean GCE and drying the electrode naturally at room temperature.

An MPI-E electrochemistry & ECL workstation (Remex Electronic Instrument Co., Ltd., Xi’an, China) was used to simultaneously record ECL response and cyclic voltammograms. The electrochemical cell consisted of the t-GQD-NAs-modified GCE as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) reference electrode, a Pt wire counter electrode and a glass cup containing electrolyte. An Eclipse Ti-U inverted microscope (Nikon, Japan) combined with an IsoPlane 160 spectrometer and a ProEM HS EMCCD camera (Princeton Instruments) working at a cooling temperature of −60 °C was used to record ECL spectra of t-GQD-NAs and ECL images of t-GQD-NAs-modified GCE. An FEI Talos F200S G2 microscope, a Verios G4 UC field emission scanning electron microscope and an Angilent 5500 atomic force microscope were adopted to obtain TEM, SEM and AFM images of nanomaterials, respectively. An ESCA LAB 250 system (America VG company), and a Renishaw Raman microscope (InVia Reflex, UK) with an excitation wavelength of 532.0 nm was operated for collecting XPS and Raman spectra of the samples, respectively.

The raw SWCNTs cannot disperse well in water even with the help of ultrasonic wave (bottle (a) in Fig. S1). In contrast, t-GQD-NAs-1, t-GQD-NAs-2 and t-GQD-NAs-3 obtained by etching SWCNTs with concentrated HNO3 for different time (8–20 h) can easily form homogeneous suspension in water (bottles (b)-(d) in Fig. S1).Probably, these carbon nanomaterials might bear abundant hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups (e.g. –COOH) at their surfaces under the strong oxidation of HNO3 [46], which decreases the surface tension of suspension [47], and increases their surface wettability in water [48].

TEM images show that raw SWCNTs are long (>2 μm) and arranged into SWCNT bundles (Fig. 2 A) and the diameter of a single SWCNT is around 1 nm (Fig. S2). After 8 h treatment of SWCNTs with HNO3, the obtained t-GQD-NAs-1 are shorter but do not have obvious change in bundle size (Fig. 2B). When the SWCNTs are etched with HNO3 for 12 h, the resultant t-GQD-NAs-2 have both much shorter tube length (see (a) in Fig. 2C) and much smaller tube bundle (see (b) and (c) in Fig. 2C). Over etching of SWCNTs with the oxidizing acid (i.e. treatment with HNO3 for 20 h) produces t-GQD-NAs-3 that consist of aggregated graphene nanoribbons (Fig. 2D), which means that SWCNTs are broken with long-time etching.

Fig. 3 shows SEM images of raw SWCNTs (A), t-GQD-NAs-1 (B), t-GQD-NAs-2 (C) and t-GQD-NAs-3 (D) corresponding to acid-etching time of 0, 8, 12 and 20 h, respectively. The SEM images clearly indicate carbon nanotube bundles become shorter and slimmer as increasing etching time until the carbon nanotubes are completely broken into graphene ribbons. Obviously, the results of SEM are consistent with those of TEM. The result that the carbon nanotube bundles become shorter can be attributed to the “cutting effect” of un-uniform etching of nanotube bundles, which can be verified by AFM image (Figure S3). The production of slimmer carbon nanotube bundles after HNO3-treatment may be associated with the forming of oxygen-containing groups (mainly –COOH) at the surfaces of t-GQD-NAs as demonstrated below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Defects and oxygen-containing groups on t-GQD-NAs
ce:section.ce:para: Raman spectrum is a valid technology to detect defects in various graphitic nanostructure [49,50]. Therefore, Raman spectra were recorded for t-GQDs-NAs to investigate the defects in these carbon nanomaterials. t-GQDs-NAs keep the D and G bands of SWCNTs around 1350 and 1590 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 4 ). The former is associated with the presence of disorder in carbon systems, while the latter can be assigned to in-plane vibration of the CC bond [51]. The intensity ratio of D to G peaks (i.e. ID/IG) reflects defect intensity in SWCNTs and t-GQDs-NAs, i. e. the larger ID/IG value, the higher defect intensity. The raw SWCNTs have a relatively low defect intensity with ID/IG value of 0.22, which means that the SWCNTs have a few defects before etching. These defect sites are quite important for effective etching of SWCNTs to produce t-GQDs-NAs, since it has been found that the defect domains can be enlarged at the initial defect sites on SWCNTs [52]. Apparently, compared with raw SWCNTs (curve (a) in Fig. 4), t-GQDs-NAs have relatively high defect intensities with ID/IG ranging from 0.31 to 0.98 (curves (b) to (d) in Fig. 4). Moreover, the longer etching time is applied, the larger ID/IG value can be obtained, showing a defect intensity ascending order: raw SWCNTs < t-GQDs-NAs-1 < t-GQDs-NAs-2 < t-GQDs-NAs-3. Additionally, a shoulder D′ band around 1620 cm−1 (attached to G band) related to defective graphite-like materials [53], is raised with increasing the etching time of SWCNTs. Briefly, Raman data show that new surface defects can be produced during HNO3-etching of SWCNTs, and those t-GQDs-NAs obtained with longer etching time have a larger defect intensity. Apparently, HNO3-etching may produce many graphitic carbon edges in GQDs-NAs by forming more or larger holes on the walls of the carbon nanotubes [52], and by cutting SWCNTs into shorter ones (as indicated by TEM and SEM images). These increased carbon edges have abundant dangling bonds (i.e. surface detects), which results in that the surface defect density of t-GQDs-NAs is obviously higher than that of raw SWCNTs.

Besides defects, the variety in oxygen-containing groups were investigated and discussed for t-GQDs-NAs. XPS survey spectra show that the raw SWCNTs are mainly composed of carbon atoms with a large C1S peak at 284.5 eV except for a small amount of oxygen atoms with a O1S at 531 eV (see curve (a) in Fig. 5 A). The O1S contents are obviously raised in the etched products with the following order: t-GQDs-NAs-1 < t-GQDs-NAs-2 < t-GQDs-NAs-3 (curves (b) to (d) in Fig. 5A), which suggests that longer HNO3-etching can result in more oxygen-containing groups. High-resoution XPS for C1s were measured and shown in Fig. 5B. The C1s XPS of raw SWCNTs and t-GQDs-NAs all have a large and sharp peak at 284.5 eV, indicating that these carbon materials are mainly made of SP2 carbons (i.e. graphitic structures). Additionally, all t-GQDs-NAs rather than the raw SWCNTs have a characteristic peak of carboxyl at 288.8 eV (curves (b) to (d) in Fig. 5B), which means that the oxygen-containing groups in t-GQDs-NAs are mainly COOH. These carboxyl groups on t-GQDs-NAs can be deprotonated into negatively charged groups (i.e. –COO-) in neutral media, and make t-GQDs-NAs (i.e. HNO3-treated SWCNTs) have zeta potential more negative than -55 mV [54]. The resultant strong electrostatic repulsion among carbon nanotubes can overcome the van der Waals interaction responsive for the formation SWCNT bundles [55], then larger SWCNT bundles will be divided into smaller ones by HNO3 oxidation. This is why t-GQDs-NA bundles are slimmer than those of raw SWCNTs.

So far, the above morphology (TEM and SEM) and optical (XPS and Raman spectroscopy) characterizations have given us some useful information that the etching have led to significant changes in morphology, and defects and oxygen-containing groups have obviously raised. However, the SWCNTs are too small (<1 nm in diameter) for TEM, SEM or AFM to see the etched “holes” and connected GQDs on these t-GQDs-NAs. Herein, we carry out ECL investigation to verify the existence of GQDs on t-GQDs-NAs, since ECL is a very useful tool for probing surface states of quantum dots [56], and is also an important property of GQDs [35,57–59]. Typically, t-GQDs-NAs-2 prepared from SWCNTs by using medium-etching time were modified on the working electrode and their ECL activity was investigated. The system containing t-GQDs-NAs-2 and S2O82- has a strong cathodic ECL emission in a wide potentialdomain from −1.1 to −2.5 V (the red curve in Fig. 6 A) with the maximum ECL peak at −2.0 V, whereas the system only containing t-GQDs-NAs-2 (the blue curve in Fig. 6A) or S2O8 2− (the green curve in Fig. 6A) has very weak ECL peaks. This suggests that t-GQDs-NAs-2 have strong ECL activity in the presence of S2O8 2−. The ECL behaviors of t-GQDs-NAs-2 are quite similar to those of GQDs [35,57]. Simultaneously recorded cyclic voltammetry (CV) shows that the electron injection into t-GQDs-NAs-2 is an irreversible process since no cathodic peak can be observed for the t-GQDs-NAs-2-modified GC electrode in the absence of S2O8 2− (blue curve in Fig. 6B). The reduction peak of S2O8 2− shifts from −1.5 V (green curve in Fig. 6B) to −0.5 V (red curve in Fig. 6B) after the GC electrode is modified with t-GQDs-NAs-2, implying that t-GQDs-NAs-2 catalyze the reduction of S2O8 2− into SO4 2− and SO4
−. The latter, i.e. SO4
− free radical is essential for the ECL of t-GQDs-NAs-2. However, the ECL peak is not found at −0.5 V where SO4
− can be largely produced, but appears at −2.0 V. This means that the injection of electron into the conduction band of t-GQDs-NAs-2 needs a relatively high reduction potential and thus determine the ECL potential of t-GQDs-NAs/ S2O8 2− system. It is difficult to measure the potential of electron injection into t-GQDs-NAs-2 directly from the CV (blue curve in Fig. 6B), but it can be known from the onset potential of ECL (red curve in Fig. 6A) that electrons begin to be injected into t-GQDs-NAs-2 at potential < −1.1 V. For comparison, the ECL behaviors of the three t-GQD-NAs and raw SWCNTs are shown In Fig. 7 . It can be known that raw SWCNTs almost have no any ECL emission (black curve), whereas t-GQD-NAs-1 (blue curve), t-GQD-NAs-2 (red curve) and t-GQD-NAs-3 (green curve) all have good ECL activities. Apparently, etching of SWCNTs into t-GQD-NAs is necessary for producing ECL. In other words, raw SWCNTs have no ECL activity, but they can have ECL emission after being changed to GQDs or their analogues. Among the ECL materials, t-GQD-NAs-2 have the highest ECL activity, which suggests that appropriate etching of SWCNTs benefits for obtaining t-GQD-NAs with good ECL. Consequently, t-GQD-NAs-2 were chosen for further ECL investigation.

The effects of some experimental conditions, such as the concentration of S2O8 2− and solution pH, on the ECL of t-GQD-NAs-2 were investigated (Fig. S4). The ECL intensity of t-GQD-NAs-2 increases with raising the concentration of S2O8 2− over the range from 0.05 to 0.2 M (Fig. S4A), showing that S2O8 2− is involved in the ECL reactions. The increase of ECL intensity tends to be slowed down when the concentration of S2O8 2− is higher than 0.1 M. Considering the limit of K2S2O8 solubility in water and the slowed down ECL intensity at higher K2S2O8 concentration, 0.1 M K2S2O8 was used for investigating ECL of t-GQD-NAs-2, unless stated otherwise. The pH effect on t-GQD-NAs-2/S2O8 2− ECL was investigated over the range of pH 4.0–10.0 (Fig. S4B). The experimental result shows that the ECL system has the brightest light emission in neutral medium (pH 7.0). Apparently, the use of pH 7.0 not only can acquire high ECL signal, but also may enable t-GQD-NAs-2 ECL material to be applied in biosensing, where biomolecules are pH-sensitive and have good activity in neutral media.

Under above optimized experimental conditions, the ECL emission spectrum of t-GQD-NAs-2 on the GC electrode was measured on our homemade ECL spectrum analyzer which consists of a microscope, a spectrometer and an EMCCD camera (for details, refer to the section of Instrumentation and Measurements). The t-GQD-NAs-2-modified GC electrode has a black disk surface in a bright field (image (a) in Fig. 8 A). The black disk outline of the modified GC electrode without applied voltage (i.e. open circuit) becomes blurry but still can be visualized by the EMCCD in dark field (image (b) in Fig. 8A). In contrast, the modified GC surface emits bright light when the electrode is applied with a cathodically scanning potential from 0 to −2.50 V (image (c) in Fig. 8A). The ECL spectrum of t-GQD-NAs-2 on the GC surface recorded by the homemade ECL spectrum analyzer is shown in Fig. 8B. The obtained ECL spectrum shows that t-GQD-NAs-2 give rise to a infrared light with a maximum emission at 695 nm, and the emission covers a wide wavelength domain, ranging from 440 nm to 940 nm (i.e. 500 nm in width). Apparently, t-GQD-NAs-2, i.e. GQD assemblies have much longer emission wavelength and broader ECL spectrum compared with free GQDs. The latter usually have maximum ECL emission around 570 nm and spectral width less than 300 nm [57–59].

The significant red-shift (Δλ = 125 nm) in maximum emission wavelength means that the energies of dominant surface states (1.78 eV, corresponding to 695 nm) on t-GQD-NAs are much lower than those on free GQDs (2.18 eV, corresponding 570 nm), and the widened emission wavelength band implies that the surface states of the assembled GQDs with energy varying from 1.32 to 2.82 eV (940–440 nm) are more abundant than those of free GQDs with a energy range of 1.65–2.76 eV (750 nm to 450 nm) [58].

Summarily, we can use Fig. 1 to depict the porous structure of t-GQD-NAs (the top section) and ECL emission of one single t-GQD-NAs on GC electrode (the bottom section). The ECL reactions of t-GQD-NAs with S2O8 2− are shown in Eqs. (2)–(5):
(2)
t-GQD-NAs
→
+
e -
t-GQD-NA
s
· -
(3)

O 8
2 -
→
+

S
O 4
2 -

O 4

(4)
t-GQD-NA
s
· -
+ S

t-GQD-NA
s *

(5)

t-GQD-NAs + hν (695
nm)

Tubular graphene quantum dot-nanoassemblies (i.e. t-GQD-NAs) can be prepared by etching SWCNTs with concentrated nitric acid. The morphological characterization results by TEM and SEM show that t-GQD-NAs are much shorter and slimmer than SWCNTs. XPS provides the information that t-GQD-NAs have abundant oxygen-containing groups, mainly existing in the form of –COOH. Raman spectra suggest that surface defect intensity of t-GQD-NAs is obviously higher than that of raw SWCNTs. t-GQD-NAs have strong cathodic ECL emission with a near-infrared wavelength (695 nm) and a broad spectrum, verifying that t-GQD-NAs consist of SP3 carbon-connected GQDs of various sizes and graphene nanoribbons. The work would be of considerable significance for giving a new insight into the oxidation of SWCNTs by nitric acid and the structure of so called SWCNT oxide, providing a new and promising ECL material for sensing and displaying, and an attractive catalyst for electrocatalyical and photocatalytical reactions.

Ruina Wang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation. Ailing Li: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Yun Huang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Yanfang Shi: Formal analysis, Investigation. Yuwu Chi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675027), the Program for Scientific and Technological Innovation Leading Talents in Fujian Province of China, and the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT_15R11), Dr. Ruina Wang and Yanfang Shi thanks the financial support from Quanzhou Science and Technology Administration, Fujian, China (Grant No. 2016Z058).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.146432.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
